intro: It rarely makes the front page, because progress is slow and bad news is loud. But look at it decade by decade and the trend is unmistakable: across the long arc, humanity is getting healthier, safer, more literate and less poor. Here's the proof — in the numbers.

metrics.title: Extreme poverty
metrics.blurb: Share of the world living on less than ~$2.15/day. It has fallen by more than three-quarters in a single generation.
metrics.source: World Bank / Our World in Data

metrics.title: Child mortality
metrics.blurb: Share of children who die before their fifth birthday. It has fallen in every decade since 1960 — from nearly 1 in 5 to fewer than 1 in 25.

metrics.title: Child deaths per year
metrics.blurb: The number of children under five who die each year, most from preventable causes — down by well over half since 1990.

metrics.title: Maternal mortality
metrics.blurb: Mothers who die from pregnancy or childbirth, per 100,000 births. Down by more than 40% since 1990 as care has reached more women.

metrics.title: Life expectancy
metrics.blurb: How long a newborn can expect to live, worldwide. It has risen in nearly every decade since 1950.
metrics.source: UN WPP / Our World in Data

metrics.title: Childhood immunization
metrics.blurb: Share of infants getting the standard three-dose DTP vaccine. From just 1 in 5 in 1980 to more than 4 in 5 today.

metrics.title: Adult literacy
metrics.blurb: Share of adults who can read and write. Nearly nine in ten people can today — and the share keeps climbing.

metrics.title: Access to electricity
metrics.blurb: Share of the world with electricity at home. More than two billion people have gained access since 1990.
metrics.source: World Bank / Our World in Data

metrics.title: Homicide in Western Europe
metrics.blurb: Murders per 100,000 people — the very long view. Everyday lethal violence has collapsed to a tiny fraction of medieval levels.
